questions.question: Differentiate between the hierarchical and network data models, providing a real-world example for each.
keyPoints: Hierarchical model organizes data in a tree-like structure with parent-child relationships (e.g., file system, XML).
Network model uses a graph structure allowing many-to-many relationships (e.g., road network, airline routes).
Key difference: hierarchical is limited to one-to-many, network supports many-to-many via set and link concepts.
Both are early data models used in legacy systems; they lack flexibility and declarative query languages.

questions.question: Explain the three-schema (external, conceptual, internal) architecture of a DBMS and describe how it provides data independence.
keyPoints: External schema describes user view; conceptual schema integrates all user views into a single logical representation; internal schema defines physical storage details.
Three-schema architecture separates user interaction from physical storage, enabling logical and physical data independence.
Logical data independence: changes to conceptual schema do not affect external schemas; physical data independence: changes to internal schema do not affect conceptual schema.
The architecture also facilitates security, as each external view can have its own access controls.

questions.question: Describe the relational data model, covering its fundamental concepts such as tables, tuples, attributes, keys, and integrity constraints. Then contrast it with the object-oriented data model, focusing on data representation, encapsulation, inheritance, and query capabilities.
keyPoints: Relational model represents data as tables (relations) with rows (tuples) and columns (attributes); primary key uniquely identifies rows; foreign key maintains referential integrity.
Integrity constraints: domain constraints, entity integrity, referential integrity, and user-defined constraints; enforced by the DBMS.
Object-oriented model treats data as objects, combining attributes and methods; supports encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism; uses OQL for queries.

Key differences: relational uses declarative SQL and set-based operations, while OO uses complex types, nested structures, and navigation via object references; relational is suited for structured business data, OO for complex, richly structured data like CAD or multimedia.

questions.question: A university database must store information about students, courses, enrollments, and instructors. Using the relational model, design an appropriate schema with tables, primary keys, foreign keys, and integrity constraints. Additionally, explain how the three-schema architecture would be applied to this system, describing the external, conceptual, and internal levels and the role of each DBMS component (storage manager, query processor, transaction manager).
keyPoints: Schema design: Student(StudentID PK, Name, DOB, Major), Course(CourseID PK, Title, Credits), Instructor(InstructorID PK, Name, Dept), Enrollment(StudentID FK, CourseID FK, Semester, Grade, PRIMARY KEY (StudentID, CourseID, Semester)).
Referential integrity enforced via foreign keys linking enrollment to student, course, and instructor tables; additional constraints such as CHECK for grade values.
External level: separate views for administrators, faculty, and students (e.g., student view sees only their enrollments; faculty view sees courses they teach).

Conceptual level: integrated relational schema describing all entities and relationships; internal level: physical storage details (indexes on StudentID, CourseID, B+ tree structures, disk pages). DBMS components: storage manager handles I/O, buffer management, file organization; query processor parses, optimizes, and executes SQL; transaction manager ensures ACID properties, concurrency control, and recovery.
